tech.headline: Solar recycling needs material diversity for viability
tech.summary: Researchers from Australia and Poland found end-of-life solar module recycling unviable without considering material diversity. The study focused on upcycling aluminium, glass and solar cells for reuse in manufacturing.

The project aims to validate low-emission production and address risks like electricity costs and electrolyzer durability.

learn.title: Floating Solar Farms on Water
learn.intro: Placing solar panels on reservoirs, lakes, and ponds captures sunlight while reducing water evaporation. This dual benefit boosts renewable generation and conserves precious water—critical for agriculture and drinking supplies as climate stress grows.
learn.c1_text: Floating arrays shade the water surface, cutting evaporation by up to 70 %. Imagine a 1‑hectare lake losing 1 m³/day; a solar cover can save roughly 700 m³ of water each day—enough for 140 families.
learn.c2_text: Water cools the panels, raising efficiency by 5–10 % compared with land installations. In sunny regions, a 100 MW floating plant can produce an extra 10 MW·h per day, translating to roughly 1 % more annual output.
learn.c3_text: India’s 500 MW floating solar project on the Banas River (Rajasthan) saves an estimated 3 million m³ of water annually and feeds power to 1 million homes, aligning with the National Solar Mission’s 100 GW target by 2030.
learn.stat3: Evaporation reduction: up to 70 % under panels

learn.title: Floating Offshore Wind for Deep Water
learn.intro: Fixed‑bottom wind turbines are limited to shallow seas. Floating platforms unlock the strongest, most consistent winds beyond 60 m depth, dramatically expanding the offshore wind resource and supporting deep‑water economies.
learn.c1_text: Tension‑leg or spar platforms anchor turbines with mooring lines, like a kite tethered to the sea floor. This design lets 12‑MW turbines operate in 100‑m‑deep water, where wind speeds average 9 m/s—10 % more power than shallow sites.
learn.c2_text: Floating turbines can be assembled onshore and towed out, cutting installation time by 30 %. The modular design also simplifies maintenance: crews can disconnect a single turbine without affecting the whole array.
learn.c3_text: India’s proposed 2‑GW floating wind park off Gujarat’s coast would tap 150‑km‑deep waters, supporting the National Offshore Wind Policy (2023) that aims for 30 GW offshore capacity by 2030.
learn.stat3: Average wind speed at 100 m depth: 9 m/s

learn.title: Lithium‑Ion Battery Storage Basics
learn.intro: Lithium‑ion batteries store electricity from renewables and release it when demand peaks. Understanding how they work is key to grasping modern grid flexibility and the shift toward a carbon‑free power system.
learn.c1_text: Inside each cell, lithium ions shuttle between a graphite anode and a lithium‑metal oxide cathode during charge/discharge. Think of it as a reversible elevator moving ions up and down, delivering 150–250 Wh/kg of energy.
learn.c2_text: Utility‑scale batteries (10–100 MW) smooth solar variability, provide frequency regulation, and enable peak‑shaving. A 50 MW/200 MWh system can power 40,000 homes for 4 hours, offsetting 30 % of a typical day's load.
learn.c3_text: India’s 1 GW/2 GWh battery fleet, announced in 2023, supports the 450 GW renewable target. Policies like the “Hybrid Renewable Energy Parks” incentivize co‑location of solar, wind, and storage to reduce curtailment.
learn.stat3: India: 1 GW battery capacity under construction (2024)

learn.title: Understanding Capacity Factor
learn.intro: Capacity factor reveals the real productivity of power plants. It explains why a 100‑MW solar farm rarely generates 100 MW continuously, helping planners compare technologies and forecast emissions.
learn.c1_text: Capacity factor = (actual energy produced ÷ maximum possible energy) × 100 %. A 100‑MW plant running at full power all year would have 100 % CF; solar typically sits around 20 %.
learn.c2_text: Weather, maintenance downtime, and grid curtailment affect CF. For wind, site wind speed (10 m/s vs 6 m/s) can double CF from 30 % to 60 %, illustrating the importance of site selection.
learn.c3_text: India’s solar farms average a 19 % CF, while new hybrid solar‑wind parks target 30 % CF, boosting land use efficiency and supporting the 2030 renewable goal.

learn.title: Green Steel with Hydrogen
learn.intro: Traditional steelmaking emits 1.8 t CO₂ per tonne of steel. Replacing coal with hydrogen in blast furnaces cuts emissions dramatically, paving the way for a low‑carbon industrial sector.
learn.c1_text: In a direct‑reduced iron (DRI) furnace, hydrogen reacts with iron ore to produce molten iron and water vapor. One kilogram of H₂ replaces roughly 3 kg of coal, eliminating CO₂ from the reduction step.
learn.c2_text: Hydrogen must be supplied at 200–300 °C and at 30–40 % purity. Scaling up requires electrolyzers powered by renewables; a 1‑Mt steel plant needs ~300 MW of clean hydrogen production capacity.
learn.c3_text: India’s 2024 “Green Steel Mission” targets 5 Mt of hydrogen‑based steel by 2030, with pilot projects in Gujarat using 100 MW solar‑electrolyzer clusters to feed Tata Steel’s DRI units.
learn.stat1: Global steel CO₂ intensity: 1.8 t CO₂/t steel
learn.stat4: India: Goal 5 Mt green steel by 2030

learn.title: Direct Air Carbon Capture with Renewables
learn.intro: Removing CO₂ straight from the atmosphere tackles emissions that are hard to eliminate at source. Pairing DAC with renewable electricity makes the process carbon‑negative and scalable.
learn.c1_text: Large fans draw ambient air through chemical sorbents that bind CO₂. The sorbent is heated (≈ 100 °C) to release pure CO₂, which is then compressed for storage or utilization—similar to a giant air‑filter that you can empty.
learn.c2_text: DAC plants consume 1.5–2 GWh of electricity per tonne of CO₂ captured. Supplying this with offshore wind or solar reduces lifecycle emissions to below 0.1 t CO₂ per tonne captured, achieving net negative removal.
learn.c3_text: A 2024 pilot in Gujarat couples a 10‑MW solar farm with a 2‑kt CO₂/yr DAC unit, demonstrating a cost of $350/tonne—aligned with India’s Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) pathway for 2030 net‑zero ambition.
learn.stat2: Energy demand: ~ 2 GWh per tonne CO₂
learn.stat3: India: Goal 10 Mt DAC removal by 2050
